title: Fermi-Dirac machines as quantizations of neurons
authors: Alexander He, Nana Liu, Mark M. Wilde
abstract: Fermi-Dirac machines were proposed recently as an approach to solving semidefinite optimization problems on quantum computers. Here, we reinterpret them as canonical quantizations of classical neurons. By viewing a classical neuron as an activation function applied to a parameterized classical Hamiltonian, we quantize this model by replacing classical variables with operators whose eigenvalues encode their possible values. This follows the standard approach to canonical quantization in quantum mechanics. Crucially, when the Hamiltonian consists of commuting operators, our construction reduces exactly to a classical neuron. More generally, our approach yields an activation observable, defined as an activation function applied to a parameterized quantum Hamiltonian. The output of this quantized neuron is a random variable with expectation value equal to that of the activation observable with respect to an input state. We develop efficient hybrid quantum-classical algorithms for evaluating outputs and gradients of our quantized neurons, enabling evaluation and training. These algorithms rely on basic primitives that include random sampling, Hamiltonian simulation, and the Hadamard test. We also quantize a whole host of other activation functions, including the smooth rectified linear unit (ReLU), sigmoid linear unit, Gaussian-smoothed ReLU, and Gaussian error linear unit (GeLU), which are known to be useful for deep learning applications. Numerical experiments indicate that neurons based on quantum Hamiltonians can learn functions that classical neurons cannot. We further define a computational decision problem based on Fermi-Dirac neurons and prove that it is BQP-complete, providing complexity-theoretic evidence against efficient classical simulation. Finally, we generalize our approach to continuous quantum variables and sketch two different ways of composing these neurons into networks.
summary: This paper proposes a way to turn classical neural network architectures into quantum-native models by quantizing the neuron's activation function. It provides a rigorous mathematical framework and efficient hybrid algorithms for training these networks, proving that they possess a computational complexity that makes them hard to simulate classically.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a framework for quantizing classical neurons into 'Fermi-Dirac machines' and providing efficient hybrid quantum-classical algorithms for their training and evaluation.
main_result: The authors prove that a decision problem based on these neurons is BQP-complete, providing evidence against efficient classical simulation, and demonstrate that these neurons can learn functions that classical neurons cannot when processing quantum data.
method: The approach uses canonical quantization to promote classical Hamiltonians to quantum operators, employing functional calculus, Hamiltonian simulation, and the Hadamard test to develop hybrid algorithms for gradient estimation and objective function evaluation.
model_or_system: The model consists of parameterized quantum Hamiltonians (including second-order interactions like the Heisenberg or transverse-field Ising models) where activation functions (e.g., Fermi-Dirac, SiLU, GeLU) are applied to the Hamiltonian operator.
key_observables: The activation observable phi(H_Q(theta)) and its expectation value with respect to an input quantum state rho.
important_parameters: Temperature (T) controlling activation sharpness, weights (w), bias (b), and interaction coefficients (Omega, omega).
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonians are assumed to be k-local, and the advantage is specifically tied to the presence of quantum input data (entangled or non-orthogonal states).
figures_summary: Figure 1 depicts the conceptual transition from classical to quantized neurons; Figure 2 shows quantum circuits for gradient and objective function estimation; Figure 3 illustrates a general quantum convolution algorithm.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the quantization framework, defines the physical model and activation functions, develops hybrid algorithms for estimation and gradients, provides complexity-theoretic proofs of BQP-completeness, and concludes with numerical experiments and generalizations to continuous variables.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: This figure illustrates one of the main conceptual contributions of our paper. We quantize various common activation functions [31, 32], including hyperbolic tangent, softplus, and sigmoid linear unit (SiLU), by following the canonical quantization procedure established by the founders of quantum mechanics. Classically, an activation function φ(x) acts on the output x = wT z + b of a classical energy function (Hamiltonian), where z is a vector input to the neuron. We replace the classical Hamiltonian with a parameterized quantum Hamiltonian H(θ) = P

FIG. 3: Quantum circuit used in the quantum convolution algorithm (Algorithm 3). The state |q⟩⟨q| of the control qumode is defined in (32), and ρ is the input state on which we would like to perform the simulation. The measurement of the control qumode at the end is a momentum-quadrature measurement giving an outcome p ∈R. The algorithm finally outputs r(p), and Theorem 3 guarantees that the expected value of the output is equal to Tr[s(A)ρ], where s = q ∗r.
Figure 3 depicts the quantum circuit used in Algorithm 3.

FIG. 4: Quantum circuit for simulating the firing of a Fermi–Dirac neuron using a single sample of the state ρ. The temperature T/2 = T1T2, where T1, T2 > 0, as detailed in Algorithm 4. The state |ℓT1⟩⟨ℓT1| of the control qumode is defined in (40), and ρ is the input state on which we would like to perform the simulation. The measurement of the control qumode at the end is a momentum-quadrature measurement giving an outcome p ∈R. The algorithm finally outputs +1 if p ≥0 and −1 otherwise.

FIG. 5: Quantum circuit used in the quantum convolution and multiplication algorithm (Algorithm 6). The states |q1⟩⟨q1| and |q2⟩⟨q2| of the control qumodes are defined in (81), and ρ is the input state on which we would like to perform the simulation. The measurements of the control qumodes at the end are momentum-quadrature measurements, giving outcomes p1, p2 ∈ R. The algorithm finally outputs r1(p1)r2(p2), and Theorem 9 guarantees that the expected value of the output is equal to Tr[s1(At1)s2(At2)ρ], where si = qi ∗ri, for i ∈{1, 2}.
